title: Femtosecond tunneling spectroscopy of ultrafast band bending dynamics at the atomic limit
authors: Vedran Jelic, Kaedon Cleland-Host, Stefanie Adams, Mohamed Hassan, Austin Hayes, Tyler L. Cocker
abstract: Atomic-scale disorder shapes the potential energy landscape traversed by photoexcited charge carriers, while the carriers themselves also dynamically reshape this landscape. However, resolving ultrafast photocarrier motion at atomic length scales has remained a central challenge in materials science. Here, we demonstrate that lightwave-driven terahertz scanning tunneling microscopy (THz-STM) provides access to these dynamics by probing the ultrafast evolution of local electronic structure following resonant interband excitation. Applying this approach to the photoexcited GaAs(110) surface, we image the resulting femtosecond carrier dynamics by tracking the transient photocurrents produced by ultrafast shifts in the energy alignment of surface and bulk electronic states near individual surface defects. Supported by modeling, we experimentally resolve the time-dependent band bending produced by photoinduced charge carriers across the atomic-scale landscape of the sample surface. Crucially, we employ terahertz time-domain spectroscopy in the tip near-field to disentangle the coherent sub-cycle dynamics induced by the terahertz driving field from the intrinsic sample response. We establish a new regime of ultrafast tunneling spectroscopy that captures transient electronic structure and dynamic band alignment with unprecedented spatio-temporal resolution, which has significant implications for understanding carrier transport, defect-mediated processes, and the development of optoelectronic technologies based on dynamically tunable materials.
summary: This paper presents a new regime of ultrafast tunneling spectroscopy using lightwave-driven THz-STM. By using an optical pump and a THz probe, the researchers can image how photoexcited carriers dynamically reshape the electronic landscape at the atomic scale. They successfully disentangle different physical processes, such as charge rectification and photocurrent modulation, near surface defects. This technique provides unprecedented spatio-temporal resolution for studying carrier transport and band alignment in semiconductors.
main_problem: Resolving ultrafast (femtosecond to picosecond) photocarrier motion and the resulting dynamic reshaping of the potential energy landscape at atomic length scales.
main_result: Demonstrated that lightwave-driven THz-STM can track transient electronic structure and time-dependent band bending near individual surface defects with unprecedented spatio-temporal resolution.
method: An optical-pump/THz-probe configuration using femtosecond near-infrared pulses and a strong-field THz pulse, combined with THz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) to disentangle coherent field dynamics from sample response.
model_or_system: The physical system is an n-type silicon-doped GaAs(110) surface containing atomic-scale defects like gallium vacancies. The experimental platform is lightwave-driven terahertz scanning tunneling microscopy (THz-STM) coupled with 3D Poisson solver (SEMITIP) simulations.
key_observables: Transient photocurrents, time-dependent band bending, rectified charge (Q_THz), differential rectified charge (delta Q_THz), and time-dependent differential conductance.
important_parameters: Femtosecond to picosecond temporal scales; atomic-scale spatial resolution; peak THz electric fields around -100 V/cm; photocurrent lifetimes ranging from ~580 fs to 910 fs.
main_assumptions: The THz waveform shape remains invariant under photoexcitation, preventing the study of the evolving dielectric function; the junction remains in the tunneling regime without reaching field emission.
figures_summary: Fig 1: STM/THz-STM topography and defect distribution; Fig 2: Poisson simulations of rectification, modulation, and electron capture; Fig 3: Lateral band bending and tip-defect distance dependence; Fig 4: 2D THz-STS maps and extracted time-dependent differential conductance.
paper_structure: Introduction of the challenge in resolving ultrafast dynamics; description of the THz-STM experimental setup and THz-TDS waveform measurement; presentation of simulation results for physical mechanisms (rectification, modulation, capture); experimental demonstration of tracking band bending at defects; and discussion of extracted carrier lifetimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Optical pump – terahertz probe multidimensional nanospectroscopy of gallium arsenide. (A) Visualization of the pulse sequence incident on the metallic STM tip (top) together with the defect-induced band-bending landscape (middle) and simultaneously recorded terahertz-induced tunnel current, QTHz(x,y) (bottom). The sequence consists of a femtosecond near-infrared pump pulse with energy εpump ∝ R |Epump|2dt, where Epump is the pump electric field. The pump center wavelength is 810 nm and its duration is 50 fs. A strong-field terahertz probe pulse with field ESF(t) is delayed by τpump relative to the pump pulse. A weak-field terahertz pulse with incident field EWF(t), arrives with a...

Fig. 2. Terahertz-pulse-induced rectification, sub-cycle photocurrent modulation and electron capture. (A) Simulated band diagrams, produced with the Poisson solver SEMITIP, for terahertz-field-induced (ETHz) rectification (red arrows) on the pristine surface with Vd.c. = 3.0 V and VTHz,pk = 4.0 V. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) are plotted with (red curve) and without (black curve) the terahertz field present, where εF,B is the Fermi level of the bulk and εF,tip is the Fermi level of the tip. (B) Simulated band diagram for terahertz-pulse-induced rectification near a negatively charged defect (VGa) with Vd.c. = 1.1 V and VTHz,pk = 1.9 V. The red arrow shows...

Fig. 3. Ultrafast manipulation of the band-bending landscape. (A) Schematic and simulated band diagram of lateral tip-induced band bending around a negatively charged (VaGa) defect. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edges at the surface are shifted by the combined fields of the tip and charged defect at Vd.c. = 2.0 V. (B to D) Schematics (top) and simulated band diagrams (bottom) with the tip 10 nm (B), 1 nm (C), and 0 nm (D) away from a negatively charged defect at Vd.c. = 2.0 V and VTHz,pk = −0.3 V. The tip’s Fermi level alignment with the C4 surface state (SS) maximizes the photocurrent at a lateral distance of 1 nm from the negatively charged defect. (E) Simultaneously...

Fig. 4. Sub-cycle differential conductance spectroscopy of an atomic defect. (A and B) Simultaneously acquired STM topography and THz-STM images of two gallium vacancies (dark depressions) and an adsorbed atom (bright protrusion) on the silicon-doped GaAs(110) surface measured in constant-current mode with τpump = 0.5 ps, ETHz,pk = −80 V/cm, εpump = 50 pJ, Vd.c. = 2.4 V, and Id.c. = 30 pA. Image size 6 × 6 nm2; scan speed 1 nm/s. (C) THz-STM image of the same area acquired at τpump = 3.5 ps with other parameters the same as (B).
